perturbation.original_text: the Receiving Party agrees that, for a period of eighteen months from the date of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall cause its affiliates, subject to Section 22 hereof, not to), directly or indirectly, (i) use the Confidential Information to divert or attempt to divert any business or customer of the Company or (ii) employ or solicit, or initiate contact for employment with, any (A) director, officer or other senior or key employee of the Company or (B) any other employee of the Company whom the Receiving Party meets during its evaluation of the possible Transaction or about whom the Receiving Party receives Confidential 2 Information; provided , however , a general advertisement or other recruiting efforts not specifically targeting any such employees of the Company shall not be considered a solicitation or unauthorized hiring.
perturbation.changed_text: the Receiving Party agrees that, for a period of six months from the date of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall cause its affiliates, subject to Section 22 hereof, not to), directly or indirectly, (i) use the Confidential Information to divert or attempt to divert any business or customer of the Company or (ii) employ or solicit, or initiate contact for employment with, any (A) director, officer or other senior or key employee of the Company or (B) any other employee of the Company whom the Receiving Party meets during its evaluation of the possible Transaction or about whom the Receiving Party receives Confidential 2 Information; provided , however , a general advertisement or other recruiting efforts not specifically targeting any such employees of the Company shall not be considered a solicitation or unauthorized hiring.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-solicitation period to six months may be unreasonably short and contradict legal standards regarding adequate protection of trade secrets and confidential information, depending on the specific nature of the information and the industry. The original duration was 18 months, now it's 6 months.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law on trade secrets and unfair competition may consider a six-month non-solicitation period insufficient to protect the Company's legitimate business interests, depending on the nature of the confidential information.
perturbation.law_citation: N.Y. Comp. Codes R. & Regs. Tit. 12 § 142-2.13
perturbation.law_explanation: While this regulation doesn't explicitly state a minimum non-solicitation period, New York courts often evaluate the reasonableness of such restrictions based on factors like the employee's access to confidential information and the time needed to dissipate that information. A six-month period may be deemed unreasonably short to protect the employer's legitimate interests, especially considering the senior roles mentioned (directors, officers, key employees).

perturbation.justification: The change from an 18-month to a 6-month non-solicitation period may contradict legal standards for adequate protection of trade secrets and confidential information. The Perturbation Explanation states the reduced period may be "unreasonably short" to protect confidential information depending on the industry. While the scraped snippets do not directly specify timeframes for non-solicitation agreements, the Law Explanation states that NY courts consider factors like the employee's access to confidential information when determining reasonableness. Thus, reducing the period to 6 months creates a potential legal contradiction due to the risk of insufficient protection of trade secrets, particularly for senior employees.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents or materials prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect any Confidential Information shall be destroyed. Notwithstanding the return to the Company or destruction of Confidential Information pursuant to this Section 7, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by their 4 confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement for the term hereof. Notwithstanding the foregoing, (a) the Receiving Party’s legal department and/or outside counsel may keep one copy of the Confidential Information (in electronic or paper form) and, with respect to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who are accounting firms, such firms may keep one copy of the Confidential Information, in each case, if required to comply with applicable law or regulation and (b) the Receiving Party and its Representatives may retain Confidential Information to the extent it is “backed-up” on its or their (as the case may be) electronic information management and communications systems or servers, is not available to an end user and cannot be expunged without considerable effort; provided, that any such information so retained pursuant to clauses (a) and (b) of this Section 7 shall be held in compliance with the terms of this Agreement for a period of eighteen months from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents or materials prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect any Confidential Information shall be destroyed. Notwithstanding the return to the Company or destruction of Confidential Information pursuant to this Section 7, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by their 4 confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement for the term hereof. Notwithstanding the foregoing, (a) the Receiving Party’s legal department and/or outside counsel may keep one copy of the Confidential Information (in electronic or paper form) and, with respect to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who are accounting firms, such firms may keep one copy of the Confidential Information, in each case, if required to comply with applicable law or regulation and (b) the Receiving Party and its Representatives may retain Confidential Information to the extent it is “backed-up” on its or their (as the case may be) electronic information management and communications systems or servers, is not available to an end user and cannot be expunged without considerable effort; provided, that any such information so retained pursuant to clauses (a) and (b) of this Section 7 shall be held in compliance with the terms of this Agreement for a period of six months from the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the retention period for backed-up confidential information to six months may violate regulations like those under the SEC or FINRA, which require longer retention periods for certain financial and business records.
perturbation.contradicted_law: SEC Rule 17a-4(b)(4) requires broker-dealers to preserve records for at least three years in an easily accessible place, and for longer periods for other types of records.
perturbation.law_explanation: SEC Rule 17a-4(b)(4) mandates a minimum three-year retention period for certain records by broker-dealers. Shortening the retention period in the confidentiality agreement to six months would violate this rule if the confidential information included records subject to SEC regulations.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 - Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 - Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.17a-4 Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. This section applies to the following types of entities: A member of a national securities exchange who transacts a business in securities directly with others than members of a national securities exchange ; a broker or dealer who transacts a business in securities through the medium of a member of a national securities exchange ; a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer as that term is defined in § 240.3b-12 , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78 o ); a security-based swap dealer registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78 o -10) that is also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act; and a major security-based swap participant registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act. Section 240.18a-6 (rather than this section) applies to the following types of entities: A security-based swap dealer registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is not also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act; and a major security-based swap participant registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is not also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act. (a) Every member , broker or dealer subject to § 240.17a-3 must preserve for a period of not less than 6 years, the first two years
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the retention period for confidential information from eighteen months to six months. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest that this may violate SEC regulations, specifically SEC Rule 17a-4(b)(4), which mandates a minimum three-year retention period for certain records. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 pertains to records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. Therefore, reducing the retention period to six months directly contradicts the potential requirement of a three-year retention period for records subject to SEC regulations.

perturbation.original_text: The Company agrees (a) that the meeting date for its 2012 annual stockholders meeting (with respect to the Company’s fiscal year ended January 28, 2012) will be held no earlier than thirty-one days after the first anniversary of the Company’s 2011 annual stockholders meeting (with respect to the Company’s fiscal year ended January 29, 2011) and (b) to provide the Receiving Party a copy of the press release announcing the date of its 2012 annual meeting on the date such press release is publicly issued.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company agrees (a) that the meeting date for its 2012 annual stockholders meeting (with respect to the Company’s fiscal year ended January 28, 2012) will be held no earlier than three hundred and ninety days after the first anniversary of the Company’s 2011 annual stockholders meeting (with respect to the Company’s fiscal year ended January 29, 2011) and (b) to provide the Receiving Party a copy of the press release announcing the date of its 2012 annual meeting on the date such press release is publicly issued.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the annual stockholders meeting by such a long period violates Delaware law, which mandates that annual meetings be held within 13 months of the previous one.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware General Corporation Law Section 211(c) requires an annual meeting of stockholders to be held for the election of directors.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware General Corporation Law Section 211(c) mandates that if an annual meeting is not held within 13 months after the latest of the organization of the corporation or its last annual meeting, the Court of Chancery may summarily order a meeting to be held upon the application of any stockholder. The modified text attempts to delay the annual meeting far beyond this 13-month limit, thus violating Delaware law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 211 Â§ 212 Â§ 213 Â§ 214 Â§ 215 Â§ 216 Â§ 217 Â§ 218 Â§ 219 Â§ 220 Â§ 221 Â§ 222 Â§ 223 Â§ 224 Â§ 225 Â§ 226 Â§ 227 Â§ 228 Â§ 229 Â§ 230 Â§ 231 Â§ 232 Â§ 233 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter VII. Meetings, Elections, Voting and Notice Â§ 211. Meetings of stockholders. (a) (1) Meetings of stockholders may be held at such place, either within or without this State as may be designated by or in the manner provided in the certificate of incorporation or bylaws, or if not so designated, as determined by the board of directors. If, pursuant to this paragraph or the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws of the corporation, the board of directors is authorized to determine the place of a meeting of stockholders, the board of directors may, in its sole discretion, determine that the meeting shall not be held at any place, but may instead be held solely by means of remote communication as authorized by paragraph (a)(2) of this section. (2) If authorized by the board of directors in its sole discretion, and subject to such guidelines and procedures as the board of directors may adopt, stockholders and proxyholders not physically present at a meeting of stockholders may, by means of remote communication: a. Participate in a meeting of stockholders; and b. Be deemed present in person and vote at a meeting of stockholders, whether such meeting is to be held at a designated place or solely by means of remote communication, provided that (i) the corporation shall implement reasonable measures to verify that each person deemed present and permitted to vote at the meeting by means of remote communication is a stockholder or proxyholder, (ii) the corporation shall implement reasonable measures to provide such stockholders and proxyholders a reasonable opportunity to participate in the meeting and to vote on matters submitted to the stockholders, including an opportunity to read or hear the proceedings of the meeting substantially concurrently with s
perturbation.justification: The modified text delays the annual stockholders meeting to three hundred and ninety days, which exceeds the legally permissible 13-month timeframe for holding annual meetings as indicated by the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly state the 13-month rule, the Law Explanation references Delaware General Corporation Law Section 211(c), and the Perturbation Explanation states that the change violates Delaware law. The altered text introduces a clear contradiction by setting a deadline that violates state law, making the terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Except as otherwise specified herein, the obligations of the parties set forth in this Agreement shall terminate and be of no further force and effect eighteen months from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as otherwise specified herein, the obligations of the parties set forth in this Agreement shall terminate and be of no further force and effect one month from the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Setting the agreement to terminate in just one month is an unreasonably short time to protect confidential information, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws that protect such data for a longer period.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York's trade secret law requires reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of information and provides remedies for misappropriation, implying a longer period of protection than one month would typically afford.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 24 of the New York Labor Law
perturbation.law_explanation: While Article 24 of the New York Labor Law doesn't specify a time limit for trade secret protection, it does establish legal recourse for misappropriation as long as the information remains a trade secret. Limiting the confidentiality agreement to one month undermines the ability to enforce those protections for any meaningful duration, effectively negating the intended protection of trade secrets and potentially conflicting with the underlying purpose of trade secret law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination period of the agreement to one month. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state that this timeframe is insufficient to protect confidential information and trade secrets, undermining the purpose of trade secret law. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly state a required duration for trade secret protection, the Law Explanation states that it establishes legal recourse for misappropriation as long as the information remains a trade secret. By limiting the confidentiality agreement to one month, the changed text effectively negates the intended protection of trade secrets, creating a legal contradiction as per the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition.
